title: Unauthorized Access Point on the IT/OT Boundary
narrative:
An automated port scan of the IT/OT boundary firewall for ExampleCorp's pipeline control network has flagged TCP port 3389 (RDP) open inbound. The scan ran at 02:15 UTC as part of the nightly compliance check. PIPELINE-DC01 and three OT workstations on the 10.0.0.0/24 segment are behind this boundary device.

In OT environments: an open inbound RDP port on a boundary firewall that faces the IT network is a critical risk — an attacker who pivots through it can directly reach historian servers and SCADA workstations on the 10.0.0.0/24 segment. Pipeline control system availability depends on this boundary being correctly enforced.

phases.title: Discovery
phases.prompt: Port 3389 inbound is open on the boundary firewall according to the overnight scan. What is your first action?

options.text: Block port 3389 immediately using New-NetFirewallRule without further investigation.
options.outcome: Blocking immediately without investigation risks disrupting an authorized remote maintenance window already in progress — you could cut off an OT vendor or authorized admin mid-session. TSA network segmentation requirements require documented responses; taking containment before establishing whether this is authorized means your incident record will lack the investigation step. Investigate first.

options.text: Check the firewall change log and verify with the change manager whether an authorized change window is active for port 3389.
options.outcome: Correct. Verifying authorization first establishes whether this is a legitimate maintenance window or an unauthorized change. If authorized, the port will close when the window ends and no further action is needed — you document the verification. If no change window is active, you have confirmed it is unauthorized and can proceed to containment with full documentation.

phases.title: Containment
phases.prompt: Investigation confirms no authorized change window is active. A rogue workstation connected to the OT VLAN opened the port 72 hours ago outside the change management process. What is the correct response?

options.text: Isolate the rogue workstation from the network, block port 3389 on the boundary firewall using New-NetFirewallRule, and notify TSA per the incident reporting requirements.
options.outcome: Correct. Isolating the device stops further exposure. Blocking the port closes the access path on the boundary firewall. Notifying TSA is mandatory — the current TSA pipeline security directive requires prompt incident notification when a cybersecurity incident or unauthorized change is identified. Document the full timeline: when the port was opened, when it was discovered, and all actions taken.

options.text: Quietly close port 3389 using New-NetFirewallRule and log it in the internal change system without notifying TSA.
options.outcome: Closing the port is correct, but not notifying TSA is a compliance violation. The current TSA pipeline security directive requires incident reporting for unauthorized changes to network access controls — handling it internally is not a compliant option when an unauthorized network change on a pipeline system is confirmed. Internal logging alone does not satisfy the TSA incident reporting mandate.
